did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) within a Federated Learning (FL) framework to classify skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The study achieved a test accuracy of 0.9816 for diagnosing pigmented skin lesions (skin cancer context).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results using a Federated Learning framework with CNNs on the HAM10000 dataset to diagnose skin cancer. It includes specific methodology (dataset preprocessing, model architecture, FedML platform usage), results (test accuracy of 0.9816 for centralized CNN, 90.1% for FL), and analysis of communication costs and accuracy trade-offs, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Privacy Preserved Federated Learning for Skin Cancer Diagnosis
journal: 2023 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Power, Electronics and Computer Applications (ICPECA)
brief_summary: This study addresses the data silo problem in skin cancer diagnosis by implementing a privacy-preserving Federated Learning (FL) framework using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers utilized the FedML platform to simulate distributed training across multiple clients with non-IID data distributions, employing preprocessing techniques like SMOTE to handle class imbalance. The primary evaluation compared the FL model's performance against a centralized CNN baseline, measuring accuracy and communication costs. The centralized CNN achieved a test accuracy of 0.9816, while the federated model with five clients reached 90.1% accuracy, representing an 8% sacrifice in performance. The results demonstrate that the FL framework effectively breaks down data silos while maintaining acceptable diagnostic accuracy for pigmented skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Seven different skin lesion classes (Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Malignant Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a CNN to classify pigmented skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset into 7 discrete diagnostic categories (labels 0-6). The model achieves a test accuracy of 0.9816 for the centralized CNN and 90.1% for the federated model. The final layer outputs classification scores for these 7 classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatological images released in 2018 to address the lack of resources for skin cancer diagnosis. It contains pigmented skin lesion images labeled with seven different skin diseases. The dataset was used as the primary data source for training and testing the CNN and Federated Learning models in this study.

use.training: The dataset was pre-processed (outlier removal and SMOTE oversampling) and randomly assigned to different clients in the FedML platform to simulate non-IID data distribution for federated training. It was also used for centralized CNN training.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the centralized CNN model (achieving 0.9816 accuracy) and the federated CNN model (achieving 90.1% accuracy).
use.other: The dataset was oversampled using SMOTE to create 35,420 patterns (5060 per class) to handle class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using accuracy and loss metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: Linear regression was used as a baseline comparison. It achieved an accuracy of about 0.69 with some losses, false positives, and false negatives. The CNN model succeeded on more than 4300 times for each category compared to linear regression's 1000-3000 correct results.

preprocessing_techniques: Outlier removal using the Stats method of the Scipy library to filter pixels with over 100 points out of 2352 pixels
Feature selection using the KBest method of the sklearn library to select the best 100-pixel features among 28x28x3 pixels
Synthetic Minority Oversampling (SMOTE) to handle class imbalance by generating new samples for minority classes based on Euclidean distance
Data extraction of label and RGB from PNG images using torchvision
Data conversion from CSV to JSON format for FedML framework compatibility

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to handle class imbalance by generating new samples for minority classes. The algorithm randomly selects two nearest minority class samples and generates a new sample within the range between them based on Euclidean distance. This increased the dataset size from 10,015 to 35,420 patterns (5,060 per class).

conclusions: The proposed Federated Learning (FL) framework using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) effectively addresses the data silo problem in skin cancer diagnosis by allowing joint model training without sharing private patient data.
The centralized CNN model achieved a test accuracy of 0.9816 (98.16%) on the HAM10000 dataset.
The federated CNN model with five clients achieved a test accuracy of 90.1%, representing an 8% sacrifice in performance compared to the centralized baseline.
The accuracy loss of less than 8% indicates that the CNN FL model in the FedML framework is feasible for information-sensitive areas.
As the number of participating clients increases, the model accuracy converges to that of a uniformly trained global model.
When the number of clients is too small, model accuracy is lower due to the limited amount of data available per client.

Due to data imbalance, the effectiveness of initial iterations decreases as the number of nodes increases, though global model results eventually approach centralized training results after a certain number of iterations.
Increasing the number of nodes leads to higher communication costs and higher total time to complete the task.
Communication latency is a significant factor, with high communication costs being the price for achieving client data privacy protection.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the FedAvg algorithm's limited effect on non-IID data scenarios, which are common in reality.
The study acknowledges that distributed system heterogeneity (poor network and hardware conditions) can reduce training efficiency in traditional federated learning.
The study acknowledges that traditional federated learning requires all edge nodes to participate in communication for each aggregation round, consuming significant communication costs.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is a public, retrospective dataset; results may not generalize to real-world clinical settings with different patient populations or imaging devices.
The paper acknowledges that the FedAvg algorithm has limited effectiveness on non-IID data distributions, which are common in real-world federated scenarios.
The federated model achieved 90.1% accuracy, representing an 8% sacrifice compared to the centralized CNN baseline (98.16%), which the authors deem acceptable for privacy preservation.
The study uses SMOTE oversampling to handle class imbalance, increasing the dataset size from 10,015 to 35,420 patterns, which may introduce synthetic bias.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between models.
The simulation assumes idealized conditions for the FedML platform and does not fully account for the impact of heterogeneous hardware and poor network conditions on training efficiency.
Communication costs and latency are identified as significant constraints, as traditional federated learning requires all edge nodes to participate in every aggregation round.
